title: Utility & indifference analysis
summary:
## The big idea Why does the demand curve slope down? At AS you took it on trust. A2 opens the bonnet: consumers buy things for the satisfaction — **utility** — they deliver, and the way utility behaves as you consume more of something explains demand itself, the water–diamond paradox, and (via indifference curves) lets you split any price change into two distinct forces. ## Total and marginal utility - **Total utility (TU)** — all the satisfaction from consuming a given quantity. - **Marginal utility (MU)** — the *extra* satisfaction from **one more unit**. The two are linked by the most human law in economics — the **law of diminishing marginal utility**: as you consume more of a good, each extra unit eventually adds *less* satisfaction. The first cold drink on a hot day is bliss; the fourth is routine; the seventh is a chore. So MU falls as quantity rises — and when MU hits zero, TU has reached its peak: one more unit beyond that actually *reduces* satisfaction.
**What each part means:** the **red TU curve** rises steeply at first — early units add lots of satisfaction — then flattens as extras add less and less. The **yellow MU curve** *is that flattening made visible*: it falls steadily (the law of diminishing marginal utility). At quantity **Q\*** marginal utility reaches **zero** — and that is exactly where **total utility peaks**. Past Q\*, MU would be negative and TU would fall.
**From MU to the demand curve:** a rational consumer keeps buying while the satisfaction of one more unit is worth its price. Since MU falls with quantity, the price they're willing to pay for extra units falls too — a falling MU curve *is* a downward-sloping demand curve in disguise. **The equi-marginal principle:** with many goods and a fixed budget, utility is maximised when the **last dollar spent on each good buys the same marginal utility**: > **MUa / Pa = MUb / Pb = …** If bananas give more utility per dollar than biscuits, shift spending to bananas; their MU falls (diminishing MU) until the ratios equalise — consumer equilibrium. **The paradox of value:** why is water (essential) cheap and diamonds (frivolous) dear? Because price tracks **marginal**, not total, utility: water is so abundant its *marginal* unit is nearly worthless, while diamonds are scarce so the marginal diamond is precious. Total utility says water wins; the market prices the margin. ## Indifference curve analysis Cardinal utility (numbers of 'utils') is a fiction; **ordinal** utility — the ability to *rank* bundles — is all we need. - An **indifference curve** joins all combinations of two goods giving the consumer **equal satisfaction**. It slopes **down** (less of one needs more of the other), is **convex to the origin** — because of the **diminishing marginal rate of substitution (MRS)**: the more pizza you already have, the less cola you'll sacrifice for another slice — curves **never cross**, and **higher curves = more utility**. - The **budget line** shows what's affordable: its position depends on **income**, its slope on the **ratio of prices**. Income change → **parallel shift**; a change in one price → **pivot**. - **Consumer equilibrium**: the highest indifference curve the budget can touch — the **tangency point**, where **MRS = price ratio**. Same idea as equi-marginal, drawn geometrically. ## Income and substitution effects When a price falls, two things happen at once, and indifference analysis can pull them apart: - **Substitution effect** — the good is now *relatively* cheaper, so consumers switch toward it. **Always** works opposite to the price change. - **Income effect** — the price fall stretches real income; the consumer can afford more of everything. For a **normal good** this adds to the substitution effect. For an **inferior good** it pushes the other way (richer consumers buy less of it) — demand still slopes down, just less steeply. In the extreme — a **Giffen good**, a strongly inferior staple dominating a poor household's budget — the income effect *overwhelms* substitution and demand can slope **upward** (the fabled rice/bread case; rare and data-shy, which is itself an evaluation point). (**Veblen goods** — status goods desired *because* they're expensive — break the law of demand by a different, snob-value route.) ## Why it matters This chapter supplies the microfoundations examiners love to probe: derive demand from diminishing MU, state the equi-marginal condition, draw the tangency, and decompose a price change. It also hands behavioural economics (Unit 6) its target — real consumers only approximate this rational machinery.
## ⚡ Quick revision - **MU** = extra utility of one more unit; **diminishing MU** → falling willingness to pay → **downward demand curve**. - **TU peaks where MU = 0.** - **Equi-marginal principle**: MUa/Pa = MUb/Pb = … at consumer equilibrium. - **Paradox of value**: price follows *marginal* utility (water cheap, diamonds dear). - **Indifference curves**: downward, convex (diminishing **MRS**), never cross; budget line slope = price ratio; equilibrium at **tangency (MRS = price ratio)**. - Price change = **substitution effect** (always opposite to price) + **income effect** (normal: reinforces; inferior: opposes; **Giffen**: overwhelms → upward demand).

keyTerms.definition: The overall satisfaction gained from consuming a given quantity of a good.
The additional utility gained from consuming one more unit of a good.
The principle that extra units eventually yield less additional satisfaction.
Utility is maximised when marginal utility per dollar is equal across all goods.
A curve showing combinations of two goods that give equal total utility.
The rate at which a consumer will swap one good for another while staying equally satisfied.
The combinations of two goods a consumer can afford given income and prices.
The change in demand due to a good becoming relatively cheaper or dearer.
The change in demand due to a price change altering real income.
A strongly inferior good whose demand rises as its price rises (income effect dominates).

diagrams: **Total & marginal utility curves:** TU rising at a decreasing rate then possibly falling; MU falling and cutting the x-axis where TU peaks.
**Indifference map + budget line:** convex indifference curves and a straight budget line; mark the tangency point as consumer equilibrium (MRS = price ratio).
**Income & substitution effects:** show a price fall pivoting the budget line, then decompose the move into substitution (along the original curve) and income (to the new curve) effects.
tips: Explain the **downward demand curve via diminishing marginal utility** — a key link between the two ideas.
State the **equi-marginal condition (MU/P equal across goods)** for consumer equilibrium.
For indifference curves, justify the **convex shape with a diminishing MRS** and note curves never cross.
Decompose a price change into **substitution (always opposite to price) and income effects**, then classify the good (normal/inferior/Giffen).
Use the **water–diamond paradox** to distinguish total from marginal utility — a neat evaluation point.

channels.name: EconplusDal
channels.why: Clear on income/substitution effects and Giffen goods.

revisionNotes:
# Utility & indifference analysis — quick revision

**Utility basics**
- TU = total satisfaction; MU = extra from one more unit; **TU max where MU = 0**.
- **Law of diminishing MU** → each unit worth less → demand curve slopes down.
- **Equi-marginal principle**: MUa/Pa = MUb/Pb (= consumer equilibrium).
- **Paradox of value**: water high TU, low MU → cheap; diamonds the reverse.

**Indifference curves**
- Equal-utility combinations; downward-sloping, **convex** (diminishing **MRS**), never cross, higher = better.
- **Budget line**: income fixes position (parallel shifts); prices fix slope (pivots).
- **Equilibrium**: tangency — MRS = price ratio.

**Income & substitution effects of a price fall**
- Substitution: relatively cheaper → buy more (always opposite to price change).
- Income: real income up → normal good: buy more (reinforces); inferior: buy less (opposes).
- **Giffen good**: strongly inferior staple; income effect wins → demand slopes up (rare!).
- **Veblen good**: bought *because* expensive (status) — different mechanism.

**Exam habits**
- Derive demand from diminishing MU explicitly.
- Justify convexity via diminishing MRS.
- Decompose price changes into the two effects, then classify the good.
questions:
# Practice questions — Utility & indifference analysis **Original practice questions in the Cambridge 9708 style** (not copied from real papers). Attempt each, reveal the answer, then self-mark. Links to real past papers are in the Past-paper questions panel. ## Paper 3 style — multiple choice **1.** A consumer's total utility from ice creams is: 1st = 20, 2nd = 35, 3rd = 45, 4th = 50. The marginal utility of the third ice cream is: - A — 45 - B — 15 - C — 10 - D — 5
▸ Show answer

C. MU = change in TU = 45 − 35 = 10. (The 4th would add only 5 — diminishing marginal utility in action.)

**2.** A consumer maximises utility across goods X and Y when: - A — total utility from X equals total utility from Y - B — the marginal utility of X equals the marginal utility of Y - C — marginal utility per dollar is equal for X and Y - D — spending on X equals spending on Y
▸ Show answer

C. The equi-marginal principle: MUx/Px = MUy/Py. Equal MUs (B) only suffice if prices happen to be equal.

**3.** Indifference curves are convex to the origin because of: - A — diminishing marginal rate of substitution - B — diminishing returns to scale - C — the income effect - D — rising opportunity cost of production
▸ Show answer

A. The more of a good you hold, the less of the other you'll give up for an extra unit — so the curve flattens, giving convexity.

**4.** The price of a strongly inferior staple food rises, and a poor household buys **more** of it. This is because: - A — the substitution effect outweighs the income effect - B — the negative income effect outweighs the substitution effect - C — the good is a Veblen good - D — the household is irrational
▸ Show answer

B. A Giffen good: the price rise so squeezes real income that the household abandons dearer foods and falls back on the staple — the income effect swamps the (opposing) substitution effect.

## Paper 3 style — data response > A student has \$12 a week for snacks. Fruit costs \$1 and chocolate \$2 per unit. Her marginal utility schedules are: fruit — 12, 10, 8, 6, 4, 2; chocolate — 22, 18, 14, 10, 6, 2 (for successive units). She currently buys 2 fruit and 5 chocolate. **(a)** Define 'marginal utility'. **[2]** **(b)** Show whether her current basket satisfies the equi-marginal principle. **[4]** **(c)** Find the utility-maximising basket for her \$12 and explain your reasoning. **[6]** **(d)** Assess whether utility theory gives a realistic account of how consumers actually choose. **[8]**
▸ Show mark guidance

(a) The additional utility from consuming one more unit of a good. [1+1]

(b) Current: 2 fruit + 5 chocolate = \$2 + \$10 = \$12 ✓ budget. MU/P: fruit 10/1 = 10; chocolate 6/2 = 3. Not equal → not utility-maximising: fruit gives more utility per dollar. [4]

(c) Rank MU per dollar and spend: fruit gives 12,10,8,6,4,2 per \$; chocolate gives 11,9,7,5,3,1 per \$. Best \$12: fruit(12,10,8,6) + choc(11,9) ≈ buy 4 fruit (\$4) + 3 chocolate? Check: allocating dollar by dollar — F12, C11(×2\$), F10, C9(×2), F8, F6, C7(×2)... totals: fruit 4 units (\$4) + chocolate 4 units (\$8) = \$12 with MU/P: fruit 4th = 6; chocolate 4th = 10/2 = 5 — close; 5 fruit + 3.5 choc impossible, so 4F + 4C is the whole-unit optimum (TU = 36 + 64 = 100 > current 22+70=92). Full working required. [6]

(d) For: predicts substitution toward better value; underpins demand. Against (behavioural): utils unmeasurable; bounded rationality — nobody computes MU/P; framing, habit and impulse dominate snack buying; preferences unstable. Judgement: useful approximation for aggregates, unrealistic for individual psychology. [8]

## Paper 4 style — essays **Essay 1 (a)** Explain how the law of diminishing marginal utility can be used to derive a downward-sloping demand curve. **[8]** **(b)** Assess whether the paradox of value (water versus diamonds) is fully resolved by marginal utility theory. **[12]**
▸ Show mark guidance

(a) Falling MU → willingness to pay for successive units falls → price must fall to induce extra purchases; equi-marginal condition formalises it.

(b) For: distinguishing total from marginal utility dissolves the paradox — abundance drives water's MU (and price) down. Complications: scarcity contexts flip it (water in a desert); diamonds partly Veblen-priced (status); markets also reflect supply costs and market power, not utility alone. Judgement: largely resolved, with qualifications.

**Essay 2 (a)** Explain, using indifference curves and a budget line, how a consumer reaches equilibrium. **[8]** **(b)** Discuss whether the income and substitution effects of a price change can explain the behaviour of demand for all types of good. **[12]**
▸ Show mark guidance

(a) Properties of curves and line; tangency (MRS = price ratio) as the highest attainable curve; diagram expected.

(b) Normal (both effects align), inferior (offsetting, demand still downward), Giffen (income effect dominates → upward) — decomposition handles these. Struggles with: Veblen/status goods (price enters utility directly), speculative demand (expectations), behavioural anomalies (framing, herding). Judgement: powerful framework, not exhaustive.
